title: Grammar: Agreement, Pronouns, Tense, and Mood
summary: Master subject-verb agreement, pronoun case and agreement, tense consistency, and the subjunctive mood to write with the precision expected in diplomatic correspondence.
content:
Subject-verb agreement requires the verb to match the true subject, not nearby nouns. Ignore intervening phrases: "The report, along with its appendices, was submitted." (subject = report, singular). Watch prepositional phrases: "The list of attachés is complete."

Indefinite pronouns are tricky. Always singular: each, either, neither, everyone, everybody, anyone, someone, no one, nobody. "Each of the delegates has a vote." Always plural: both, few, many, several. With none/some/all/most, agreement depends on the object: "Some of the funding is gone" but "Some of the funds are gone."

With neither/nor (and either/or), the verb agrees with the nearer subject: "Neither the ambassador nor her aides were available" but "Neither the aides nor the ambassador was available." With "there is/are," the verb agrees with the noun that follows: "There are several objections." "The number of" is singular ("The number of treaties is rising"); "a number of" is plural ("A number of nations are objecting").

Pronoun case: use subject pronouns (I, he, she, we, they, who) for subjects and object pronouns (me, him, her, us, them, whom) for objects. Test compounds by dropping the other noun: "between you and me" (not "I"); "She invited him and me." Who/whom: substitute he/him — if "he" fits, use who; if "him" fits, use whom. "Whom did you consult?" ("you consulted him"). Reflexives (myself, herself) only refer back to the subject; never use "myself" as a plain subject or object: "Send it to the director or me" (not "myself").

Pronoun-antecedent agreement: a pronoun must match its antecedent in number. Indefinite singulars take singular pronouns in formal writing: "Each member must submit his or her credentials." Avoid the vague "they" in formal contexts, or recast as plural: "All members must submit their credentials."

Verb tense consistency: keep tenses logical within a passage. Do not shift needlessly: "The envoy arrived and presented her remarks" (not "arrived and presents"). Use perfect tenses to sequence events: "By the time the summit began, the parties had agreed on an agenda."

The subjunctive mood expresses wishes, hypotheticals contrary to fact, and demands/recommendations. Contrary-to-fact: "If the treaty were ratified, relations would improve" (use "were," not "was"). After verbs of request/demand (recommend, insist, require, suggest, propose), use the base verb: "The committee recommended that the report be revised" and "We insist that he attend."
keyPoints: The verb agrees with the grammatical subject; ignore phrases between subject and verb.
Each, either, neither, everyone, anyone, no one, and similar indefinites are singular.
With neither/nor and either/or, the verb agrees with the nearer subject.
"There is/are" agrees with the following noun; "the number" is singular, "a number" is plural.
Use subject pronouns for subjects, object pronouns for objects; test compounds by dropping the other noun.
Who = he (subject); whom = him (object). Reflexives must refer back to the subject.
Pronouns must agree with antecedents in number; recast singular indefinites as plural to avoid awkwardness.
Keep verb tenses consistent; use the perfect tenses to show one event preceding another.
Use the subjunctive 'were' for contrary-to-fact conditions.
After demand/recommend/insist/require, use the base-form verb in the that-clause.

flashcards.front: The report, along with its appendices, (was/were) submitted.
flashcards.back: was — the subject is 'report' (singular); 'along with its appendices' is an intervening phrase.

flashcards.front: Each of the delegates (has/have) a vote.
flashcards.back: has — 'each' is always singular, despite the plural 'delegates.'

flashcards.front: Neither the ambassador nor her aides (was/were) available.
flashcards.back: were — with neither/nor, the verb agrees with the nearer subject ('aides').

flashcards.front: There (is/are) several objections to the proposal.
flashcards.back: are — with 'there,' the verb agrees with the following noun ('objections,' plural).

flashcards.front: The number of treaties (is/are) rising; a number of nations (is/are) objecting.
flashcards.back: is rising; are objecting — 'the number' is singular; 'a number' is plural.

flashcards.front: Correct this: She invited him and I to the briefing.
flashcards.back: She invited him and me — 'me' is the object; drop 'him and' to test: 'invited me.'

flashcards.front: (Who/Whom) did the committee consult?
flashcards.back: Whom — substitute 'him': 'The committee consulted him,' so use 'whom.'

flashcards.front: Correct this: Please forward the cable to the director or myself.
flashcards.back: ...to the director or me — 'myself' is reflexive and cannot serve as a plain object.

flashcards.front: Each member must submit (his or her / their) credentials. (formal)
flashcards.back: his or her — for strict agreement; better, recast: 'All members must submit their credentials.'

flashcards.front: Correct this: The envoy arrived and presents her remarks.
flashcards.back: ...arrived and presented her remarks — keep tense consistent (both past).

flashcards.front: If the treaty (was/were) ratified, relations would improve.
flashcards.back: were — use the subjunctive for a contrary-to-fact condition.

flashcards.front: The committee recommended that the report (is/be) revised.
flashcards.back: be — after recommend/insist/require, use the base-form verb in the that-clause.

flashcards.front: By the time the summit began, the parties (agreed/had agreed) on an agenda.
flashcards.back: had agreed — the past perfect shows the agreement preceded the summit.

flashcards.front: Between you and (I/me), the vote is close.
flashcards.back: me — object of the preposition 'between.'

title: Punctuation: Commas, Semicolons, Colons, Apostrophes, and Hyphens
summary: Use commas, semicolons, colons, apostrophes, and hyphens correctly to produce unambiguous, polished official writing.
content:
Commas have four core jobs. (1) After an introductory element: "After the summit, the parties issued a statement." (2) Around nonrestrictive (nonessential) elements: "The treaty, which was signed in 1995, remains in force." Use no commas for restrictive clauses: "The treaty that they signed in 1995 lapsed." (3) In a series: "We met envoys from France, Germany, and Spain" (the serial/Oxford comma prevents ambiguity). (4) Around appositives: "Our host, the deputy minister, welcomed us."

A comma splice joins two independent clauses with only a comma — an error. "The vote passed, the session adjourned" is wrong. Fix it: period ("...passed. The session..."), semicolon ("...passed; the session..."), comma + coordinating conjunction ("...passed, and the session..."), or subordination ("After the vote passed, the session...").

Semicolons link closely related independent clauses without a conjunction: "The talks stalled; neither side would concede." Use a semicolon before a conjunctive adverb that joins two clauses, with a comma after it: "The deadline passed; however, negotiations continued." Semicolons also separate items in a complex series when items contain internal commas: "The delegation included Paris, France; Bonn, Germany; and Rome, Italy."

Colons introduce a list, explanation, or amplification after a complete independent clause: "The agenda had three items: trade, security, and aid." Do not place a colon directly after a verb or preposition: write "The priorities are trade, security, and aid" (no colon), not "The priorities are: trade..."

Apostrophes show possession and contractions. Singular possessive: add 's ("the envoy's report"). Plural possessive of a noun ending in s: add only an apostrophe ("the envoys' reports"). Irregular plural: add 's ("the children's program"). The classic trap: its = possessive ("The nation defended its border"); it's = it is/it has ("It's a complex issue"). Apostrophes are not used to form plurals ("the 1990s," not "the 1990's").

Hyphens join compound modifiers before a noun: "a well-known diplomat," "a long-term agreement," "a high-level meeting." Do not hyphenate when the modifier follows the noun ("the diplomat is well known") or when the first word is an -ly adverb ("a carefully worded statement," no hyphen).
keyPoints: Place a comma after an introductory word, phrase, or clause.
Set off nonrestrictive (nonessential) elements with commas; restrictive elements take none.
Use the serial (Oxford) comma before 'and' in a series to avoid ambiguity.
A comma alone cannot join two independent clauses — that is a comma splice.
Use a semicolon to join two related independent clauses with no conjunction.
Before a conjunctive adverb (however, therefore, moreover) joining clauses, use a semicolon and follow it with a comma.
Use a colon only after a complete independent clause; never after a verb or preposition.
Singular possessive = 's; plural possessive of an -s noun = apostrophe only.
Its = possessive; it's = it is/it has; never use an apostrophe to form a plural.
Hyphenate compound modifiers before a noun, but not -ly adverb compounds or modifiers placed after the noun.

flashcards.front: Punctuate: After the summit the parties issued a statement.
flashcards.back: After the summit, the parties issued a statement. (comma after introductory phrase)

flashcards.front: Restrictive vs. nonrestrictive: when do you use commas?
flashcards.back: Nonrestrictive (nonessential) info takes commas: 'The treaty, which was signed in 1995, ...' Restrictive (essential) info takes none: 'The treaty that they signed lapsed.'

flashcards.front: Why use the Oxford comma in 'France, Germany, and Spain'?
flashcards.back: It separates the last item clearly, preventing ambiguity about whether items are grouped.

flashcards.front: Correct this: The vote passed, the session adjourned.
flashcards.back: Comma splice. Fix: 'The vote passed; the session adjourned.' or 'The vote passed, and the session adjourned.'

flashcards.front: When do you use a semicolon between clauses?
flashcards.back: To join two closely related independent clauses without a conjunction: 'The talks stalled; neither side would concede.'

flashcards.front: Punctuate: The deadline passed however negotiations continued.
flashcards.back: The deadline passed; however, negotiations continued. (semicolon before, comma after the conjunctive adverb)

flashcards.front: When do you use semicolons in a series?
flashcards.back: When the items themselves contain commas: 'Paris, France; Bonn, Germany; and Rome, Italy.'

flashcards.front: Correct this: The priorities are: trade, security, and aid.
flashcards.back: The priorities are trade, security, and aid. (No colon after a verb; the clause before a colon must be complete.)

flashcards.front: its vs. it's
flashcards.back: its = possessive ('its border'); it's = it is / it has ('It's complex').

flashcards.front: Possessive of one envoy vs. several envoys: the envoy___ report; the envoys___ reports
flashcards.back: the envoy's report (singular 's); the envoys' reports (plural, apostrophe only).

flashcards.front: Correct this: The summit occurred in the 1990's.
flashcards.back: ...in the 1990s. (No apostrophe to form a plural.)

flashcards.front: Hyphenate? a high level meeting / a long term agreement
flashcards.back: a high-level meeting; a long-term agreement (hyphenate compound modifiers before the noun).

flashcards.front: Hyphenate? a carefully-worded statement
flashcards.back: a carefully worded statement — no hyphen after an -ly adverb.

flashcards.front: Hyphenate? The diplomat is well-known.
flashcards.back: The diplomat is well known — no hyphen when the modifier follows the noun.

title: Usage & Word Choice: Confused Words and Concision
summary: Distinguish the most commonly confused word pairs and eliminate redundancy to keep professional writing exact and economical.
content:
Affect vs. effect: affect is usually a verb meaning to influence ("Sanctions affect trade"); effect is usually a noun meaning result ("The effect was immediate"). As a verb, effect means to bring about ("to effect change").

Its vs. it's: its is possessive ("the agency and its staff"); it's means it is or it has ("It's been a long negotiation"). Their/there/they're: their = possessive ("their proposal"); there = place or existence ("over there"; "there are reasons"); they're = they are ("they're ready"). Who's vs. whose: who's = who is/has ("Who's attending?"); whose = possessive ("the envoy whose visa expired").

Principal vs. principle: principal = main/chief (adj.) or a head person/sum of money (noun) — "the principal objection," "the school principal"; principle = a fundamental rule or belief — "a matter of principle." Mnemonic: a princiPAL can be your PAL; a principLE is a ruLE.

Complement vs. compliment: complement = to complete or go well with ("The data complement the report"); compliment = praise ("She paid him a compliment"). Mnemonic: complEment complEtes.

Ensure/insure/assure: ensure = to make certain ("Ensure the documents are signed"); insure = to provide financial coverage ("insure the shipment"); assure = to reassure a person ("I assure you the matter is handled"). You assure people; you ensure outcomes; you insure property.

Fewer vs. less: use fewer for countable nouns ("fewer delegates," "fewer errors") and less for uncountable quantities ("less funding," "less time"). "Fewer than ten amendments," but "less than an hour."

Redundancy and wordiness weaken authority. Cut filler such as "in order to" (use "to"), "due to the fact that" (use "because"), "at this point in time" (use "now"), and "in the event that" (use "if"). Avoid built-in redundancies: "advance planning" (planning is advance), "end result," "past history," "each and every," "completely eliminate," "close proximity," "final outcome." Prefer one precise word to a vague phrase: "a large number of" becomes "many."
keyPoints: Affect = verb (to influence); effect = noun (result); 'effect change' = bring about.
Its = possessive; it's = it is/it has.
Their (possessive), there (place/existence), they're (they are) are three different words.
Who's = who is/has; whose = possessive.
Principal = main or chief; principle = a rule or belief (a principLE is a ruLE).
Complement = complete/match; compliment = praise (complEment complEtes).
Assure people, ensure outcomes, insure property.
Fewer for countable nouns; less for uncountable quantities.
Replace wordy phrases: 'in order to' to 'to,' 'due to the fact that' to 'because.'
Delete built-in redundancies: 'end result,' 'past history,' 'each and every,' 'advance planning.'

flashcards.front: affect vs. effect
flashcards.back: affect = verb, to influence ('Sanctions affect trade'); effect = noun, result ('The effect was clear'). 'Effect change' = to bring about.

flashcards.front: Choose: The new policy (affected/effected) morale.
flashcards.back: affected — verb meaning influenced.

flashcards.front: its vs. it's
flashcards.back: its = possessive ('its staff'); it's = it is/it has ('It's done').

flashcards.front: their / there / they're
flashcards.back: their = possessive; there = place/existence; they're = they are.

flashcards.front: who's vs. whose
flashcards.back: who's = who is/has ('Who's coming?'); whose = possessive ('whose passport').

flashcards.front: principal vs. principle
flashcards.back: principal = main/chief or a head person/sum; principle = a fundamental rule (a principLE is a ruLE).

flashcards.front: Choose: It was a matter of (principal/principle).
flashcards.back: principle — a fundamental belief or rule.

flashcards.front: complement vs. compliment
flashcards.back: complement = complete/go with ('the wine complements the meal'); compliment = praise. complEment complEtes.

flashcards.front: assure / ensure / insure
flashcards.back: assure a person; ensure an outcome (make certain); insure property (financial coverage).

flashcards.front: Choose: Please (ensure/insure/assure) the report is filed by noon.
flashcards.back: ensure — make certain of the outcome.

flashcards.front: fewer vs. less
flashcards.back: fewer = countable ('fewer errors'); less = uncountable ('less time').

flashcards.front: Choose: We received (fewer/less) complaints this quarter.
flashcards.back: fewer — complaints are countable.

flashcards.front: Tighten: in order to / due to the fact that / at this point in time
flashcards.back: to / because / now.

flashcards.front: Fix the redundancy: We must completely eliminate the past history of delays.
flashcards.back: We must eliminate the history of delays. ('completely eliminate' and 'past history' are redundant.)

title: Syntax & Sentence Structure: Parallelism, Modifiers, and Sentence Boundaries
summary: Build balanced, well-connected sentences by enforcing parallelism, placing modifiers correctly, fixing fragments and run-ons, and comparing items logically.
content:
Parallelism means items in a series or pair share the same grammatical form. Faulty: "The role involves drafting cables, analysis of data, and to brief officials." Parallel: "The role involves drafting cables, analyzing data, and briefing officials." Correlative conjunctions (not only...but also; either...or; neither...nor; both...and) must frame parallel structures: "The plan is not only ambitious but also feasible" (adjective/adjective), not "not only ambitious but also it works."

A misplaced modifier sits next to the wrong word, distorting meaning: "The minister discussed the crisis with reporters wearing a dark suit" wrongly suggests the reporters wore the suit. Fix by placing the modifier next to what it describes: "Wearing a dark suit, the minister discussed the crisis with reporters." Watch limiting words like only: "only the council can approve" differs from "the council can only approve."

A dangling modifier has no logical word to attach to. "Having reviewed the file, the decision was clear" — who reviewed it? Fix by naming the actor: "Having reviewed the file, the panel found the decision clear." Opening -ing and -ed phrases must modify the subject that follows.

A sentence fragment lacks a subject, a verb, or completeness: "Because the talks collapsed." Fix by attaching it to an independent clause: "Because the talks collapsed, the summit was postponed." Watch fragments beginning with which, although, since, and -ing words standing alone.

A run-on (fused) sentence jams independent clauses together with no punctuation: "The vote failed the measure was withdrawn." Fix with a period, semicolon, or comma-plus-conjunction: "The vote failed, so the measure was withdrawn." (A comma alone is a comma splice — also wrong.)

Coordination vs. subordination signals relationship. Coordination (and, but, or; semicolons) joins equal ideas. Subordination (because, although, while, since, if) shows that one idea depends on or qualifies another and reveals the more important clause: "Although the offer was generous, the board declined" emphasizes the declining.

Faulty comparisons compare unlike things or leave comparisons incomplete. Illogical: "The economy of Spain is larger than France." Correct: "...larger than that of France" (economy vs. economy). Use "as...as" fully ("as strong as, if not stronger than") and complete every comparison: "This route is faster" begs "faster than what?"
keyPoints: Items in a series or pair must share the same grammatical form (all gerunds, all nouns, etc.).
Correlative conjunctions (not only...but also, either...or) must join parallel structures.
Place modifiers next to the word they describe to avoid misplacement.
An opening -ing/-ed phrase must logically modify the subject that follows, or it dangles.
A fragment lacks a subject, verb, or completeness; attach it to an independent clause.
A run-on fuses independent clauses; fix with a period, semicolon, or comma plus a coordinating conjunction.
A comma alone cannot join two independent clauses (comma splice).
Use subordination to show which idea is dependent and which is emphasized.
Compare like with like: 'the economy of Spain' with 'that of France.'
Make every comparison complete and logical ('faster than the alternative').

flashcards.front: Make parallel: drafting cables, analysis of data, and to brief officials
flashcards.back: drafting cables, analyzing data, and briefing officials (all gerunds).

flashcards.front: Fix: The plan is not only ambitious but also it works.
flashcards.back: The plan is not only ambitious but also feasible. (Correlatives must join parallel forms — adjective/adjective.)

flashcards.front: What's wrong: The minister discussed the crisis with reporters wearing a dark suit.
flashcards.back: Misplaced modifier — it sounds like the reporters wore the suit. Fix: 'Wearing a dark suit, the minister discussed the crisis with reporters.'

flashcards.front: Fix the dangler: Having reviewed the file, the decision was clear.
flashcards.back: Having reviewed the file, the panel found the decision clear. (Name the actor who reviewed it.)

flashcards.front: Fragment or sentence: Because the talks collapsed.
flashcards.back: Fragment. Fix: 'Because the talks collapsed, the summit was postponed.'

flashcards.front: Fix the run-on: The vote failed the measure was withdrawn.
flashcards.back: The vote failed, so the measure was withdrawn. (Or use a period/semicolon.)

flashcards.front: Why is 'The vote failed, the measure was withdrawn' wrong?
flashcards.back: Comma splice — a comma alone can't join two independent clauses. Use a semicolon or add 'so.'

flashcards.front: Coordination vs. subordination: which emphasizes one idea over the other?
flashcards.back: Subordination — 'Although the offer was generous, the board declined' emphasizes the main clause (the declining).

flashcards.front: Fix the comparison: The economy of Spain is larger than France.
flashcards.back: ...larger than that of France. (Compare economy with economy, not with a country.)

flashcards.front: What's incomplete: This route is faster.
flashcards.back: Faster than what? Complete it: 'This route is faster than the coastal one.'

flashcards.front: Place 'only': The council ___ can approve the budget (only the council, no one else).
flashcards.back: Only the council can approve the budget. (Place 'only' next to what it limits.)

flashcards.front: Make parallel: She is responsible for hiring, training, and to evaluate staff.
flashcards.back: ...hiring, training, and evaluating staff.

flashcards.front: Fix the dangler: After reading the memo, the points seemed valid.
flashcards.back: After reading the memo, I found the points valid. (Supply the reader.)

title: Professional Communication: Concision, Voice, Tone, and Clarity
summary: Write official correspondence that is concise, appropriately voiced, suitably formal, and structured for clarity, choosing the most effective revision in every case.
content:
Concision strengthens authority. Cut filler and hedges: "It should be noted that," "in my opinion," "basically," "very," "in terms of." Replace phrases with single words: "in order to" to "to," "a majority of" to "most," "at the present time" to "now." Wordy: "We are writing to inform you that the meeting has been scheduled for Tuesday." Tight: "The meeting is scheduled for Tuesday."

Active vs. passive voice: active is usually clearer and assigns responsibility — "The committee approved the budget" beats "The budget was approved by the committee." Use the active voice as your default. Passive is appropriate, however, when the actor is unknown, irrelevant, or deliberately de-emphasized, or to maintain diplomatic neutrality: "Mistakes were made," "The report was submitted on time," "Funds were misallocated" (where naming a culprit would be undiplomatic). Choose passive purposefully, not by habit.

Tone and formality must fit official correspondence: measured, courteous, and precise. Avoid contractions (use "do not," not "don't"), slang, and overly casual phrasing in formal letters. Prefer "We regret that we are unable to accommodate the request" over "Sorry, we can't do that." Maintain a respectful, neutral register even when delivering unwelcome news.

Nominalizations bury verbs inside nouns and add bulk. "Conduct an investigation of" should be "investigate"; "reach a decision" should be "decide"; "give consideration to" should be "consider"; "make an assessment of" should be "assess." Convert noun-heavy phrases back into strong verbs for directness.

Topic sentences orient the reader. Begin each paragraph with a sentence that states its main point, then support it. In a memo or report, lead with the conclusion or recommendation (the "bottom line up front") so busy officials grasp the message immediately.

Salutations and closings follow convention. Formal openings: "Dear Ambassador Reyes," or "Dear Sir or Madam," when the recipient is unknown; use a colon after the salutation in formal U.S. business letters ("Dear Mr. Secretary:"). Formal closings: "Sincerely," "Respectfully," or "Yours faithfully," followed by a comma and your name. Match the closing's formality to the salutation.

Most-effective-revision strategy: when choosing among versions of a sentence, prefer the one that is grammatically correct, concise, unambiguous, and consistent in tone with the surrounding text. Eliminate options with errors first, then redundancy, then awkwardness or shifts in register; the best answer says the same thing in the fewest clear words.
keyPoints: Cut filler and hedges ('it should be noted that,' 'basically,' 'very') and replace phrases with single words.
Default to the active voice; it is clearer and assigns responsibility.
Use passive deliberately when the actor is unknown, unimportant, or best de-emphasized for neutrality.
Keep official tone measured and courteous; avoid contractions, slang, and casual phrasing in formal letters.
Unbury verbs from nominalizations: 'investigate,' not 'conduct an investigation of.'
Start each paragraph with a topic sentence; put the bottom line up front in memos.
Use conventional salutations and match closing formality to the opening.
In formal U.S. business letters, use a colon after the salutation.
When picking the best revision, eliminate errors first, then wordiness, then awkwardness.
The most effective revision is correct, concise, unambiguous, and consistent in tone.

flashcards.front: Tighten: We are writing to inform you that the meeting has been scheduled for Tuesday.
flashcards.back: The meeting is scheduled for Tuesday.

flashcards.front: When is the active voice preferred?
flashcards.back: As the default — it is clearer and assigns responsibility: 'The committee approved the budget.'

flashcards.front: Name two cases where passive voice is appropriate.
flashcards.back: When the actor is unknown/irrelevant, or when de-emphasizing the actor for diplomatic neutrality ('The report was submitted on time').

flashcards.front: Make active: The budget was approved by the committee.
flashcards.back: The committee approved the budget.

flashcards.front: Unbury the verb: conduct an investigation of / reach a decision / give consideration to
flashcards.back: investigate / decide / consider.

flashcards.front: Formalize: Sorry, we can't do that.
flashcards.back: We regret that we are unable to accommodate the request.

flashcards.front: What is a topic sentence and where does it go?
flashcards.back: A sentence stating the paragraph's main point; place it first, then add support.

flashcards.front: What does 'bottom line up front' mean in a memo?
flashcards.back: Lead with the conclusion or recommendation so the reader grasps the message immediately.

flashcards.front: Correct punctuation after a formal U.S. business salutation: Dear Mr. Secretary___
flashcards.back: Dear Mr. Secretary: (colon in formal business letters).

flashcards.front: Give a formal salutation for an unknown recipient and a matching closing.
flashcards.back: 'Dear Sir or Madam,' opening; 'Yours faithfully,' or 'Sincerely,' closing.

flashcards.front: Cut the filler: It should be noted that the deadline is firm.
flashcards.back: The deadline is firm.

flashcards.front: How do you choose the most effective revision?
flashcards.back: Eliminate errors first, then redundancy, then awkwardness/tone shifts; pick the correct, concise, unambiguous, tone-consistent version.

flashcards.front: Replace with one word: at the present time / a majority of / in order to
flashcards.back: now / most / to.

flashcards.front: Tighten: Due to the fact that the vote was delayed, we will make an assessment of the impact.
flashcards.back: Because the vote was delayed, we will assess the impact.
